title: Bias-Correction Methods Reveal Underestimation by Observational Studies
author: Mahmood Ahmad
summary: Seven bias-correction methods reveal 9-13% underestimation by observational studies masked by standard meta-analysis.
body: Do standard random-effects meta-analyses mask systematic publication bias in meta-epidemiological comparisons of randomized and observational studies? We assembled 57 ratio-of-ratios comparisons from the IMPACT-HTA database and a BMC Medicine 2022 review covering cardiovascular, oncology, and infectious disease domains. Seven correction methods including REML, Hartung-Knapp, PET-PEESE, trim-and-fill, limit meta-analysis, selection models, and Bayesian MCMC were applied to each comparison against the standard uncorrected estimate. The standard estimate showed no significant bias, but after correction all convergent methods indicated 9 to 13 percent underestimation by observational studies, with median corrected RR 0.887 (95% CI 0.764 to 0.989). Selection models failed for all three specifications due to insufficient sample size, while extreme heterogeneity at I-squared 91.2 percent was driven by 35 percent discordant comparisons. Stratification by a novel discordance index revealed that 64.9 percent of comparisons show agreement below the two-fold divergence threshold. However, a limitation is that the 57-comparison sample restricts statistical power for subgroup-specific bias correction estimates.

sentences.text: Do standard random-effects meta-analyses mask systematic publication bias in meta-epidemiological comparisons of randomized and observational studies?
We assembled 57 ratio-of-ratios comparisons from the IMPACT-HTA database and a BMC Medicine 2022 review covering cardiovascular, oncology, and infectious disease domains.

Seven correction methods including REML, Hartung-Knapp, PET-PEESE, trim-and-fill, limit meta-analysis, selection models, and Bayesian MCMC were applied to each comparison against the standard uncorrected estimate.

The standard estimate showed no significant bias, but after correction all convergent methods indicated 9 to 13 percent underestimation by observational studies, with median corrected RR 0.887 (95% CI 0.764 to 0.989).
Selection models failed for all three specifications due to insufficient sample size, while extreme heterogeneity at I-squared 91.2 percent was driven by 35 percent discordant comparisons.
Stratification by a novel discordance index revealed that 64.9 percent of comparisons show agreement below the two-fold divergence threshold.
However, a limitation is that the 57-comparison sample restricts statistical power for subgroup-specific bias correction estimates.

notes.data: 57 RoR comparisons from IMPACT-HTA and BMC Medicine 2022

references: Roever C. Bayesian random-effects meta-analysis using the bayesmeta R package. J Stat Softw. 2020;93(6):1-51.
Higgins JPT, Thompson SG, Spiegelhalter DJ. A re-evaluation of random-effects meta-analysis. J R Stat Soc Ser A. 2009;172(1):137-159.
Borenstein M, Hedges LV, Higgins JPT, Rothstein HR. Introduction to Meta-Analysis. 2nd ed. Wiley; 2021.
